summary: Gruber dissects Microsoft's MSN Music help page, which blamed Apple for iPod incompatibility with Windows Media DRM while positioning Microsoft's own proprietary format as the open 'consumer choice.' He notes the hypocrisy of calling Windows Media 'popular' while labeling Apple's market-leading AAC/FairPlay as merely 'proprietary DRM.' He further reveals that Microsoft revised the help page from a genuinely useful answer (explaining how to burn and re-import music) to pure spin that offered no actual solution. The revision replaced helpful instructions with blame-shifting talking points, making the help page less useful but more aligned with Microsoft's messaging.
key_points: Microsoft selectively applied 'popular' to Windows Media and 'proprietary DRM' to AAC/FairPlay, when both descriptors apply equally to both formats — except Apple held 70% market share
Microsoft's 'consumer choice' rhetoric required Windows Media format, Windows OS, and Internet Explorer — no actual choice at all
The original MSN help page contained a genuinely useful workaround (burn to CD, reimport via iTunes) that was removed in favor of pure anti-Apple messaging
Microsoft's claim that Apple 'refuses to allow other companies to integrate' was demonstrably false, given HP's iPod hardware deal and Audible.com's partnership
money_quotes: The only one they choose for themselves — 'popular' — is the least apt for Windows Media, given that Apple's AAC/FairPlay has 70 percent market share.

There are more than 70 portable audio devices that depend on technology licensed and controlled by Microsoft today, and we hope that someday Apple gives up on their own market-leading technology, bends over, and uses ours.
The 'consumer choice' championed by Microsoft involves no choice at all regarding: Media format (Windows Media), Operating system (Windows), Web browser (Internet Explorer).
But other than that, Microsoft is all for choice.
Help pages are more useful when they're helpful, rather than spiteful.
It's no more or less standard than AAC/FairPlay, unless you subscribe to the school of thought that standards are whatever Microsoft says are standards.
key_insight: Microsoft's 'consumer choice' campaign against the iPod was pure spin — they replaced a genuinely helpful answer with blame-shifting talking points, revealing that their real goal was lock-in to Microsoft-controlled technology, not interoperability.
